sections.about: This metric measures the number of green credits generated or procured by the listed entity. It is an environmental / green finance disclosure linked to Principle 6 and BRSR Core-style environmental performance reporting, rather than a legacy Principle 7 metric. A higher value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: All companies report 0 green credits in FY2025, while FY2022-23 and FY2023-24 data is not available for any company. Therefore, there is no substantive peer differentiation in FY2025: Tata, JSW, SAIL, and Jindal are tied. The most useful interpretation is that green-credit reporting has entered the dataset, but the steel peers have not yet generated or procured credits under this metric. Jindal’s disclosure explicitly shows 0 green credits for the listed entity and 0 for top-ten value chain partners, while still disclosing operational energy-saving initiatives such as smart cooling control and DM plant optimisation.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2025, Tata ranks joint 1st at 0 because all peers report the same value. There is no performance gap from the best performer. This should not be over-positioned as leadership; it is a non-differentiating metric at present. Tata’s management focus should remain on underlying decarbonisation capex, energy efficiency, waste utilisation, water stewardship, and credible transition investments rather than claiming advantage from green credits.
sections.comparabilityNotes: FY2022-23 and FY2023-24 values are not available for any company. FY2025 values are all zero, so ranking reflects equal disclosure/outcome rather than differentiated green-credit performance.

flags.flagNote: No meaningful peer differentiation is possible because all FY2025 values are 0 and prior years are missing.
